title: Semiparametric conformal prediction
abstract: Many risk-sensitive applications require well-calibrated prediction sets over multiple, potentially correlated target variables, for which the prediction algorithm may report correlated errors. In this work, we aim to construct the conformal prediction set accounting for the joint correlation structure of the vector-valued non-conformity scores. Drawing from the rich literature on multivariate quantiles and semiparametric statistics, we propose an algorithm to estimate the $1-α$ quantile of the scores, where $α$ is the user-specified miscoverage rate. In particular, we flexibly estimate the joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the scores using nonparametric vine copulas and improve the asymptotic efficiency of the quantile estimate using its influence function. The vine decomposition allows our method to scale well to a large number of targets. As well as guaranteeing asymptotically exact coverage, our method yields desired coverage and competitive efficiency on a range of real-world regression problems, including those with missing-at-random labels in the calibration set.
extracted_text:
SEMIPARAMETRIC CONFORMAL PREDICTION
Ji Won Park
Prescient Design, Genentech
Robert Tibshirani
Department of Statistics, Stanford University
Kyunghyun Cho
Prescient Design, Genentech
Center for Data Science, New York University
March 12, 2025
ABSTRACT
Many risk-sensitive applications require well-calibrated prediction sets over multiple, potentially
correlated target variables, for which the prediction algorithm may report correlated errors. In this
work, we aim to construct the conformal prediction set accounting for the joint correlation struc-
ture of the vector-valued non-conformity scores. Drawing from the rich literature on multivariate
quantiles and semiparametric statistics, we propose an algorithm to estimate the 1 −α quantile of
the scores, where α is the user-specified miscoverage rate. In particular, we flexibly estimate the
joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the scores using nonparametric vine copulas and
improve the asymptotic efficiency of the quantile estimate using its influence function. The vine
decomposition allows our method to scale well to a large number of targets. As well as guarantee-
ing asymptotically exact coverage, our method yields desired coverage and competitive efficiency
on a range of real-world regression problems, including those with missing-at-random labels in the
calibration set.
Keywords conformal prediction · nonparametric statistics · copulas
1
INTRODUCTION
Many problems in industrial and pharmaceutical applications can be framed as multi-target regression, where the goal
is to predict a vector target taking values in Rd from some input features. In early-stage drug discovery, for example,
it is necessary to jointly characterize the ADME (absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion) properties of a
candidate molecule. The complex biophysical processes governing these properties manifest as correlations in their
measurements, often pronounced in the tails (Jain et al., 2017; Niepel et al., 2019; Du et al., 2023).
We often require prediction sets capturing uncertainties, rather than point estimates (Eklund et al., 2015; Cortes-
Ciriano and Bender, 2020). The medicinal chemists may use the uncertainties on a given molecule to decide whether
to test it in the wet lab. One way to express uncertainties is as confidence at a specified coverage level. Conformal
prediction (CP) stands out as a powerful framework that can wrap around any underlying prediction algorithm to
produce valid confidence sets for any data distribution (Gammerman et al., 1998; Saunders et al., 1999; Vovk et al.,
2005). A confidence set at the 1 −α level for a random variable Y , denoted Γ1−α, is valid if it contains the true value
of Y at least 1 −α of the time, that is, P[Y ∈Γ1−α] ≥1 −α.
Central to the CP algorithm is the concept of ranks and quantiles (Kuchibhotla, 2020). The confidence set at level 1−α
is constructed by extracting the 1−α quantile of the non-conformity score quantifying how much a prediction deviates
from the labels. In the context of split CP (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Lei et al., 2015) for single-target regression, for
instance, one may define the score as the absolute error and rank the scores evaluated on the calibration set to obtain
the 1 −α quantile, as illustrated in Figure 1(a, b).

Figure 1: Top: Single-target split CP. Green dashed line in (a) marks the 1−α quantile of calibration scores, setting the confidence
interval around test predictions in (b). Bottom: Independent split CP for two targets, with √1 −α quantiles marked by green
dashed lines in (c). Large prediction sets for two test instances shown in (d).
CP can be applied independently for each target, not accounting for the correlation in the scores (Papadopoulos et al.,
2002). Ranking then proceeds like the single-target case at a marginal level 1−αj > 1−α for each target j that would
satisfy the desired global coverage level of 1 −α. By treating the scores as independent, however, we miss out on
potential gains in efficiency, or the tightness of the prediction (Neeven and Smirnov, 2018). Figure 1(c, d) illustrates
independent CP for two-target regression. Setting 1 −αj = √1 −α marginally results in large confidence sets. In
general, the calibration set may not be large enough to accommodate extreme 1 −αj levels, forced to be closer to 1 as
d increases.
We instead model the targets jointly by viewing the target-wise scores as random vectors S that take values in Rd. The
key question then is how to define the 1 −α quantile of observed S. While ranks and quantiles in R form the basis of
nonparametric statistics (Lehmann et al., 1975), a canonical ordering does not exist in Rd for d > 1. Several notions
of multivariate quantiles have been proposed, including ones based on statistical depth (Liu, 1990; Zuo, 2003) and
geometry (Chaudhuri, 1996; Hallin et al., 2010). We build on the work of Messoudi et al. (2021) that proposed using
the copula, a statistical tool for modeling the dependence structure of multivariate random variables Nelsen (2006).
Copulas can be used to estimate the joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the scores, F(s) = P[S1 ≤
s1, . . . , Sd ≤sd], where s ∈Rd. The quantile is its generalized inverse, or the p-level curve F −1(p) = {s ∈Rd :
F(s) = p}. Figure 2 depicts the level curves of the copula (colored contours) fit on correlated scores (gray dots). For
1 −α = 0.9, the 0.9-level line contains quantiles with smaller norms compared to the independent target approach
(green dot), indicating that joint modeling can yield more efficient prediction sets.
The main novelty of our approach lies in viewing the task of determining F −1(1 −α) as a semiparametric inference
problem. Our goal is to flexibly estimate F using a nonparametric model while ensuring that inference remains robust
and precise for a particular estimand of interest, namely the quantile F −1(1 −α).
As an update to prior work, we estimate F using vine copulas (Bedford and Cooke, 2002), specifically the nonpara-
metric variant (Nagler et al., 2017) for additional expressibility. Vine copulas can scale well to many targets, as they
factorize high-dimensional densities into a product of bivariate densities. Applications of copulas for CP have previ-
ously been limited to the empirical copula or a small set of parametric families, which can be biased when observations
are sparse or the model misspecified (Messoudi et al., 2021; Sun and Yu, 2024). We also demonstrate that a particular
copula model allows working with missing-at-random (MAR) target labels in the calibration set.

Figure 2: Scores with an upper tail correlation and long-tailed marginals. The joint 0.9-quantile of the scores can yield more
efficient prediction sets than the independent

representing the 0.9 level. (c): Version of (b) viewed in the copula space.
Having inferred F, rather than simply using the plug-in estimate of the quantile, we tailor our analysis to this specific
functional by computing the one-step estimator (Le Cam, 1956) based on its influence function (Hampel, 1974), best
understood as a generalized gradient of the functional with respect to small perturbations of F. This debiased estimator
specifically targets the quantile estimand of interest and carries better theoretical properties like √n-consistency and
asymptotic normality (Hines et al., 2022).
Contributions: (1) We propose the use of nonparametric vine copulas for flexibly estimating the joint distribution
of the non-conformity scores in multivariate conformal calibration (Section 5.1). (2) We propose the semiparametric
one-step estimator for debiasing the 1−α score quantile used to construct the prediction sets (Section 5.2). (3) We
prove that our method produces prediction sets with asymptotically exact coverage and present a variant with finite-
sample distribution-free validity. (4) We empirically demonstrate the validity and efficiency of our method on various
real-world regression problems (Section 6.2).
2
RELATED WORK
A large body of work aims at extending conformalized quantile regression (Romano et al., 2019) to multiple targets
(Kong and Mizera, 2012; Paindaveine and ˇSiman, 2011; Feldman et al., 2023). There are parallel efforts to make full
CP more computationally efficient for some classes of multi-target predictors (Johnstone and Ndiaye, 2022; Nouret-
dinov et al., 2001). Our focus is to improve the calibration step without additional assumptions on the underlying
algorithm.
When there are multiple response variables, a common approach is to design a single scalar score, such as the L2
distance between the vector-valued prediction and the label (Kuleshov et al., 2018; Johnstone and Cox, 2021; Messoudi
et al., 2022; Feldman et al., 2023). This implies choosing a target aggregation scheme, however, and the resulting score
3


Semiparametric conformal prediction
PARK, TIBSHIRANI, AND CHO
is sensitive to the relative scales in the predictions and labels across targets. Moreover, the labels for all d targets need
to be observed for score evaluation, which may be impractical for large d.
Another approach is to conformalize the predictions for each individual target (Neeven and Smirnov, 2018). Non-
conformity scores are evaluated target-wise and the score at the 1 −αj quantile extracted for each target j such that
Q
j(1 −αj) = 1 −α. If all targets are to be treated equally, we set 1 −αj = (1 −α)1/d > 1 −α for all j. Note
that as d increases, (1 −α)1/d approaches 1, leading to inefficient prediction sets. Such extreme levels also require
the calibration dataset to be sufficiently large.
If the scores are highly correlated, we can exploit the dependence structure to improve efficiency. Messoudi et al.
(2021) pioneered the use of copulas for jointly modeling the distribution of vector scores. Sun and Yu (2024) applied
this idea to multi-step time series forecasting. The empirical copulas used in previous work can be unstable with sparse
observations, especially in the upper tails near the quantile level of interest. Messoudi et al. (2021) also uses simple
parametric copulas like Gumbel and Gaussian, which offer a smoother fit but can suffer from model misspecification.
Copulas are reviewed in Section 4.
3
Problem setup
Consider a multivariate regression task given a dataset {(X(i), Y (i))}i∈I of input features X(i) ∈X and vector-
valued labels Y (i) ∈Rd. The dataset can be viewed as |I| exchangeable samples drawn from the joint distribution
PXY = PX × PY |X. Given a new test point X∗and a prespecified miscoverage level α ∈[0, 1), we would like to
determine the set Γ1−α(X∗) ⊂Rd that has valid coverage.
Definition 1 (Marginal validity). A set Γ1−α is marginally valid1 at level 1 −α if it contains the response Y ∗with
probability at least 1 −α:
P[Y ∗∈Γ1−α(X∗)] ≥1 −α.
(1)
Suppose an algorithm ˆf : X →Rd predicts the multiple responses from the input. Our goal is to equip this model,
henceforward referred to as the “underlying” algorithm, with valid confidence sets. We assume ˆf to be a point predictor
throughout the main text, and Section 17 includes results for a conditional density estimator ˆf : X →Rd × Rd
+ that
outputs the mean and variance of the Gaussian predictive distribution.
4
BACKGROUND
Conformal prediction (CP)
constructs prediction sets without any assumptions on the data distribution PXY (Vovk
et al., 2005). The set is guaranteed to be marginally valid for finite samples as long as the data are exchangeable.
CP was originally formulated in the transductive setting (Gammerman et al., 1998; Saunders et al., 1999; Vovk et al.,
2005). Here we focus on split (inductive) CP (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Lei et al., 2018), where the labeled dataset
{(X(i), Y (i))}i∈I with index set I is split into the proper training data Itrain, used to fit the underlying algorithm ˆf,
and the calibration data Ical, used to conformalize it.2
Calibration involves the non-conformity score, denoted V (X, Y, ˆf), that measures the extent to which a query point
(X, Y ) deviates from the patterns learned by ˆf from the training set. Higher values indicate more disagreement.
Let us first consider the d = 1 regression case and define the score as the absolute error, V (X(i), Y (i), ˆf) = | ˆf(X(i))−
Y (i)| =: S(i) ∈R+. Given a miscoverage rate α ∈[0, 1] and a test input X∗∈X, the conformal set gathers outcomes
yielding the smallest scores:
Γ1−α(X∗) =

(6)
where v ≼w for v, w ∈Rd if v1 ≤w1 . . . , vd ≤wd. To address the remaining question of modeling the CDF ˆF of
the score vectors in Equation 3, we turn to copulas.
Copulas
are statistical tools well suited for modeling the dependence structure of multivariate random variables.
Definition 2 (Copula). Given a random vector S = [S1, . . . , Sd], denote the marginal CDF for each component
variable Sj, j ∈[d], as Fj(sj) = P[Sj ≤sj]. The copula C : [0, 1]d →[0, 1] of S is the joint CDF of U =
[F1(S1), . . . , Fd(Sd)]:
C(u1, . . . , ud) = P[F(S) ≼u].
(7)
Note that each Fj(Sj) is the probability integral transform (PIT) of Sj and follows a uniform distribution on the
interval [0, 1]. Thus the copula function joins (couples) random variables, capturing their dependence structure, after
all the complexities in their marginal distributions have been removed. The following theorem establishes its existence
and uniqueness.
Theorem 1 (Sklar’s theorem (Sklar, 1959)). If random vector S has a a joint CDF F and marginal CDFs F1, . . . , Fd,
there exists a copula C : [0, 1]d →[0, 1] such that F(s1, . . . , sd) = C(F1(s1), . . . , Fd(sd)). The copula is unique if
F1, . . . , Fd are continuous. The associated copula density is f(u1, . . . , ud) = c(F1(s1), . . . , Fd(sd))f1(s1) · · · fd(sd),
where f1, . . . , fd are the marginal densities of S.
Estimation of F therefore decomposes into estimating (1) the marginal distributions F1, . . . , Fd and (2) the copula
function C. Kernel density estimation (KDE) and the empirical CDF enable nonparametric modeling of (1). For
(2), we can choose a parametric copula family such as the Gaussian, Gumbel, and Clayton based on the observed
correlation structure and estimate the parameters with maximum likelihood or moment matching. The parametric
approach, however, is prone to model misspecification.
The vine copula, or the pair copula construction, can accommodate dependencies of arbitrary complexity when a
single standard copula model is inadequate. It is a hierarchical model composed of bivariate copula blocks organized
in a sequence of nested trees, also called regular (R-) vines or simply vines Joe (1994). The main idea is to factorize a
d-dimensional copula density into a product of d(d −1)/2 bivariate conditional copula densities. Fitting a vine then
boils down to choosing the bivariate copula models and the vine structure. The bivariate copulas can be estimated in
a nonparametric manner (Chen and Huang, 2007; Geenens et al., 2017; Nagler et al., 2017). See Section 9 for more
details on nonparametric vine copula estimation.
While nonparametric vine copulas enable flexible estimation of F, in the context of CP, we are only interested in its
quantile functional, F −1(1 −α). Semiparametric methods help tailor our inference for this particular objective.
Semiparametric inference
refers to the inference of nonparametric estimands, or functionals of the true data distri-
bution P. Also called targeted or debiased learning, this approach has emerged as a major paradigm in recent years
(Van der Laan et al., 2011). If the target functional of interest is “smooth” (in ways we will make concrete below), it
is sometimes possible to endow estimators with parametric properties like √n-consistency and asymptotic normality,
even if our estimate of P converges at a slower rate (Murphy and Van der Vaart, 2000; Van der Laan et al., 2011;
Kennedy, 2022; Hines et al., 2022).
Suppose P belongs to a set of probability measures P on a Polish sample space (Z, A). The target estimand is Ψ(P),
where Ψ : P →R is a measurable functional. We begin with the following key concept.

said to be pathwise differentiable (Van Der Vaart, 1991). The EIF serves as a first-order distributional derivative that
characterizes how sensitive Ψ(P) is to changes in P (Robins et al., 2017; Kennedy, 2022).

5
SEMIPARAMETRIC CP
As described in Section 3, choosing an underlying prediction algorithm ˆf : X →R and a target-wise non-conformity
score function V (Xj, Yj, ˆf) induces score vectors in Rd evaluated on the calibration set, defined in Equation 4 and
Equation 5. We robustly estimate their 1 −α quantile by estimating the score distribution in a flexible non-parametric
manner (Section 5.1) and applying the one-step correction to the plug-in quantile of the estimated distribution (Sec-
tion 5.2). See Algorithm 1 for the full algorithm.
5.1
Nonparametric density estimation
Motivated by recent developments in nonparametric estimation of copulas (Chen and Huang, 2007), we propose the
use of nonparametric vine copulas described in Section 4 to model the distribution F of score vectors. First, we obtain
the empirical marginal CDF:

Parameters of the copula density, including the kernel bandwidth and vine structure, are fit using the Akaike informa-

then be obtained by pairwise Monte-Carlo (MC) integration of the density. We use the pyvinecopulib library (Vatter
and Nagler, 2024) for fitting and model selection.
5.2
Semiparametric correction
Having obtained ˆF, we first compute the plug-in quantile:
Ψ( ˆF) = {s ∈Rd : ˆF(s) ≥1 −α}.
(15)
This represents the (1 −α)-level curve of ˆF illustrated in Figure 2. Note that the quantile is not unique; any quantile
on the (1 −α)-level curve will do. The choice of which quantile to use for calibration depends on our preferences on
the relative efficiency across the multiple targets. Suppose we would like a quantile with the smallest L1 norm. We
can obtain this point on the level curve by running optimization to find the quantile in the copula space and mapping
it back to the score space via the inverse PIT:
U ∗= arg min

We can then use Equation 17 again to map U19step to the score space and obtain Ψ19step( ˆF).
Figure 4 illustrates one-step estimation in the single-target case where we seek to estimate the 1 −α = 0.9 quantile
(green dashed line) of the true score distribution (green curve) based on n=20 samples. Standard CP takes the empir-
ical CDF (black curve) and extracts the ⌈(n + 1)(1 −α)⌉/(n + 1) quantile (dashed black line). Our approach is to
“augment” the scores by fitting a nonparametric model for the CDF (purple curve). Here, the plug-in quantile (dashed
purple line) lies very close to the empirical one and both overestimate the quantile, whereas the corrected estimate
(solid purple line) offers a better approximation.
The final prediction set for our method is given in Equation 6. As with Messoudi et al. (2021), it takes hyperrectangular
shapes. For V (X, Y, ˆf)j = |Yj −ˆf(Xj)|, for example, the target-wise intervals are
Γ1−α(X∗)j = {y ∈R : |y −ˆf(X∗)j| ≤Q1−α,j},
(20)
where Q1−α can be Ψ( ˆF) or Ψ19step( ˆF).
5.3
Theoretical results
We informally state the theoretical guarantees of our method below. The coverage of Algorithm 1 is asymptotically
exact by Theorem 2 and is approximately valid by Theorem 3. Complete proofs can be found in Section 13. We
assume the following:
• The true distribution F ∗over the score vectors S is absolutely continuous.
• The nonparametric copula estimator ˆF is uniformly consistent (i.e. sups∈Rd
    ˆF(s)−F ∗(s)
   
p→0 as n →∞.)
under the standard smoothness and bandwidth conditions.
Theorem 2 (Asymptotic consistency). Under the stated regularity conditions, prediction set Γ1−α from Algorithm 1
constructed using the one-step correction U ∗satisfies
P[Y ∗∈Γ1−α(X∗) →1 −α as n →∞,
for a test point (X∗, Y ∗). That is, its coverage is asymptotically exact.

The statement follows from the uniform consistency of the copula estimator ˆF, which implies consistency of its
quantile, and asymptotic normality of the one-step estimator.
Theorem 3 (Approximate validity). Suppose that the total variation (TV) distance between the true distribution F ∗
and the estimated distribution ˆF is bounded by ϵ. That is,
sup
S
|F ∗(S) −ˆF(S)| ≤ϵ.
Then the resulting prediction set Γ1−α from Algorithm 1 constructed either from the plug-in quantile U or the one-step
corrected quantile U ∗is marginally valid at the 1 −α −ϵ level:
P[Y ∗∈Γ1−α(X∗)] ≥1 −α −ϵ.
The proof proceeds as follows. By the data processing inequality, the TV distance between F ∗and ˆF upper-bounds
the TV distance between their corresponding quantiles. The same holds between the quantile of ˆF and the one-step
corrected quantile, as the correction term does not add additional information.
Finite-sample validity.
In Section 12, we also introduce Algorithm 2, a variant of Algorithm 1 that further splits the
calibration set into Ical,1 and Ical,2. The set Ical,1 is used to build the empirical CDF ˆFj, ∀j ∈[d] and the set Ical,2
with n′ = |Ical,2| is used to fit the empirical copula ˆC, from which the ⌈(1−α)(n′+1)/(n′+1)⌉quantile is extracted.
The extra split provides finite-sample validity by Theorem 5, because it makes the vector marginals U between Ical,2
and the test set exchangeable.
6
EXPERIMENTS
Setup.
We investigate split conformal calibration schemes for regression with d real-valued targets. The underlying
algorithm ˆf fit on Itrain is a multi-task Lasso point predictor (Tibshirani, 1996). For calibration schemes that operate
on vector scores, including ours, we choose the L1 non-conformity score in Equation 4. Scores are evaluated on the
9

same calibration set of size n = |Ical|. We fix 1 −α = 0.9 and randomly split the data into training, calibration, and
test sets.
Baselines.
We compare calibration schemes that vary in the score definition and ˆF estimation. Table 3 provides a
detailed comparison of all the schemes.
• univariate calibration applied independently for each target at the (1 −α)1/d level (independent)
• calibration applied to a scalar score defined as the L2 norm3 of the prediction error, V (X, Y, ˆf) = ||Y −
ˆf(X)||2 (Feldman et al., 2023)
• empirical copula fit on vector scores with the constraint U ∗
1 = · · · = U ∗
d Messoudi et al. (2021)
• TLL R-vine fit on vector scores (ours, plug-in)
• one-step correction applied to the above (ours, corrected)
Evaluation metrics.
We evaluate coverage and efficiency for each calibration scheme. Coverage is calculated as the
empirical coverage on the test set Itest:

that are efficient, or have small sizes, so we also report their (hyper)volumes:

6.1
Synthetic example
We generate data using the penicillin production simulator from (Liang and Lai, 2021). This function takes in seven
input parameters controlling penicillin production and outputs three real-valued targets (d=3), namely penicillin yield,
production time, and carbon dioxide emission. To simulate measurement errors, we add random Gaussian errors
with scale equal to 10% of the noiseless function output, so that Y = f0(X) + ε where f0 is the test function and
ε ∼N(0, (f0(X)/10)2). More details are in Section 15.1.
As reported in Table 1, our corrected method achieves coverage closest to the nominal level of 0.9 and is the most
efficient among the valid schemes. The one-step correction improves on the plug-in in terms of both coverage and
efficiency. Figure 5 shows that the coverage improvement applies for all target levels. The level curves of the true,
estimated, and empirical distributions for two penicillin targets are shown in Figure 6.
On the other hand, the independent scheme is forced to extract an empirical marginal quantile at the ⌈(0.9)1/d(n +
1)⌉/(n+1) ≈0.97 level from only n≈100 calibration instances, so become overconservative (Messoudi et al., 2021).
It cannot take advantage of the correlation in the residuals, particularly in the tails, that arise due to (tail) correlations
in the target quantities, as visualized in Figure 8 and Figure 9.
The scalar score suffers even more from inefficiency, because the L1 score aggregates residuals from targets with
drastically different distributions. In this example, the residuals for CO2 emissions have a long upper tail relative to
those for penicillin yield (see Figure 8). Thus the scores are dominated by CO2 residuals and the resulting quantile
takes a large value.
Section 14 includes a complete set of experiments including our Algorithm 2 as well as other types of scalar scores
(L2 and L∞norms) and a “split” scalar-score method that further splits the calibration set for normalizing the scores
prior to scalarization.
6.2
Real datasets
We report performance on a variety of real-world datasets with 6, 8, or 16 targets and a range of calibration-set sizes
in Table 2. In all of these problems, our corrected scheme is valid and has empirical coverage closest to 0.9. It is also
the most efficient among the valid schemes. For Stock (Liu and Yeh, 2017), Caco2+ (Wang et al., 2016; Park et al.,
3This score definition yields prediction sets shaped as d-dimensional balls. The L1 norm would yield cross-polytopes (d-
dimensional generalization of diamonds). The L∞norm would yield hypercubes.

With the other calibration schemes, we observe similar patterns as in the synthetic example. The independent method
tends to overcover, and gives infinitely large prediction sets for the Stock dataset where we only have n=63 instances
from which to extract the empirical ⌈(0.9)1/6 × 64⌉/64≈0.98 quantile. The scalar score overcovers even more and
leads to very large prediction sets. The empirical copula exhibits high variance due to limited samples in the upper
tails close to the target quantile. Its coverage falls substantially below the nominal level as well.
7
LIMITATIONS
Just like the algorithm of Messoudi et al. (2021), our algorithm only guarantees approximate finite-sample validity.
Loss of coverage can occur if the estimated copula ˆC of the scores, constructed with n data points, deviates signif-
icantly from the “true” copula in the n →∞limit. The parametric copula families considered in Messoudi et al.
(2021) is prone to model misspecification bias; we aim at minimizing this using nonparametric vines.
In our work, we used CMA-ES (Hansen et al., 2003), a gradient-free evolutionary algorithm, to optimize the copula.
This step can be made more efficient, particularly for large d, by using gradient-based algorithms. Unlike empirical
copulas and some classes of parametric copulas, which lend themselves to gradient descent algorithms (Sun and Yu,
2024), nonparametric vines are more complicated for gradient computation. We leave this to future work.
8
CONCLUSION
We improve on conformal calibration for the multi-target regression setting. When there are many targets relative to
the size of the calibration set, empirical quantile extraction suffers from curse of dimensionality and can exhibit high
variance due to limited samples in the upper extreme of the non-conformity scores. Previous approaches to learn the

joint score distribution and exploit its smoothness have been limited to parametric copula families, which are prone to
model misspecification. Our method instead specifies a nonparametric model for full flexibility, but controls the plug-
in bias on the quantile estimand of interest by way of the one-step semiparametric correction. We have demonstrated
that our method yields prediction sets with desired coverage and competitive efficiency on a range of high-dimensional
regression problems. Because it makes no assumptions about the form of the underlying algorithm or score function,
it can be plugged in easily at the calibration step.
An interesting avenue for future work may be to specify the structure of the vine based on domain knowledge about
the target dependence structure. We may use C-vines for when one target variable dominates or D-vines for temporal
variables considered in (Sun and Yu, 2024).
